column1: 31-year-old patient with abdominal pain after injury L7P2R5V9X3YC [DD1]
column3: Axial CECT of a splenic laceration with active bleeding shows a low-attenuation area of parenchymal laceration with adjacent active bleeding .
column7: CT abdomen with contrast. Based on the image, name 4 key differentials and give one best discriminator for each.
column9:
Pattern differs because: this drill trains the low-attenuation splenic defect pattern rather than the vascular-blush pattern.
Splenic laceration/fracture: irregular linear, branching, stellate, or deep nonenhancing low-attenuation defect in the setting of trauma, often with hematoma or hemoperitoneum.
Splenic cleft: thin fissure-like polar low-attenuation band without adjacent hematoma, free fluid, or stranding.
Splenic infarct: wedge-shaped peripheral hypoattenuation/nonenhancement, typically without surrounding free fluid or hematoma unless rupture occurs.
Splenic abscess: rounded irregular low-attenuation collection with infection signs; gas, when present, is highly specific.

column7: CT abdomen with contrast. Based on the image, name 3 key vascular injury possibilities and give one best discriminator for each.
column9:
Dominant pattern differs from other cases because: this drill trains focal contrast-density vascular injury rather than the parenchymal laceration itself.
Active arterial extravasation: focal high attenuation initially similar to arterial blood pool that changes in size or morphology on delayed images as blood pools outside the vessel.
Pseudoaneurysm: rounded contained vascular injury that remains similar in size on delayed imaging and has high rupture risk without treatment.
Arteriovenous fistula: contained vascular injury indistinguishable from pseudoaneurysm by CT; splenic arteriography is required to differentiate it from pseudoaneurysm.

column7: CT abdomen with contrast. Based on the image, name 3 splenic hemorrhage compartments and give one best discriminator for each.
column9:
Dominant pattern differs from other cases because: this drill trains hemorrhage compartment localization rather than laceration-vs-mimic discrimination.
Subcapsular hematoma: crescentic blood constrained by the splenic capsule, compressing or flattening the lateral splenic margin.
Perisplenic hematoma: blood adjacent to the spleen that implies disruption or rupture of the splenic capsule.
Intraparenchymal hematoma: blood collection within splenic parenchyma, typically round, ovoid, or irregular.

column16:
Source definition: Splenic parenchymal injury +/- disruption of the overlying splenic capsule.
Boundary condition: This is an organ injury pattern, not merely free intraperitoneal blood; hematoma, laceration/fracture, vascular injury, and capsular disruption determine imaging severity.
Synonyms: splenic laceration; splenic fracture.

Source definition: Deep splenic laceration extending from the outer capsule through the splenic hilum.
Boundary condition: It is deeper than a superficial laceration and implies a higher-risk central injury pattern; it is not a thin congenital cleft.
Synonyms: deep splenic laceration.

column11: Why must the spleen be evaluated for traumatic injury on portal venous phase CT?
column12: In arterial phase, physiologic heterogeneous splenic enhancement can create patchy areas that either mimic low-attenuation injury or obscure a real laceration. Portal venous phase produces more homogeneous splenic parenchymal enhancement, so a true laceration stands out as a linear or branching area of decreased/nonenhancing attenuation. The imaging pivot is that arterial phase is useful for vascular injury, but portal venous phase is the safer phase for parenchymal injury detection.

column11: Why do delayed-phase images distinguish active extravasation from pseudoaneurysm in splenic trauma?
column12: Active extravasation represents contrast leaving an injured vessel and pooling in the hematoma or peritoneal space, so the focus changes in size, shape, or attenuation on delayed imaging. A pseudoaneurysm is a contained arterial wall injury and an AV fistula is a contained artery-to-vein injury; both can appear as focal high attenuation initially, but they do not enlarge like free extravasated contrast on delayed images. The discriminator is containment versus ongoing pooling outside the vascular lumen.

column11: Why can a pseudoaneurysm after splenic trauma rupture if untreated?
column12: Core describes pseudoaneurysm as injury to the intima and media of the arterial wall, with rupture contained only by the adventitia. Because the normal arterial wall layers are disrupted and the containment is incomplete, the lesion remains a high-risk contained vascular injury rather than a stable hematoma. The imaging pivot is that a rounded contrast collection should not be dismissed as simple clot; it may require angiographic treatment even when not actively enlarging on delayed CT.

column13: What CT-phase pitfall can cause missed or overcalled splenic injury, and what safety check prevents it?
column14:
Trap: Interpreting the spleen only on arterial phase can lead to either overcalling physiologic heterogeneous enhancement as injury or missing a true laceration hidden by heterogeneity.
Safety check: Evaluate splenic parenchyma on portal venous phase, where enhancement is more homogeneous.
Key discriminator: True laceration remains a linear/branching low-attenuation defect on contrast-enhanced evaluation, while physiologic arterial heterogeneity should not be used alone to diagnose injury.
Why it happens: Arterial-phase splenic enhancement is normally heterogeneous and can visually mimic or mask trauma.

column13: What vascular-injury pitfall should trigger delayed imaging, and what discriminator resolves it?
column14:
Trap: Calling every focal contrast-density splenic focus active extravasation, or dismissing a rounded focus as benign, without checking delayed behavior.
Safety check: Add delayed-phase images when a suspicious focus is seen on initial portal venous or arterial-phase images.
Key discriminator: Active extravasation changes in size or morphology on delayed images; pseudoaneurysm/AVF remains contained and does not enlarge.
Why it happens: Active extravasation and contained vascular injuries can both initially appear as focal high attenuation.

column13: What normal variant can mimic traumatic splenic injury, and what surrounding findings prevent the error?
column14:
Trap: Mistaking a congenital splenic cleft for a traumatic laceration.
Safety check: Look for adjacent hematoma, free fluid, or stranding and assess the morphology/location of the defect.
Key discriminator: A splenic cleft is a thin fissure-like low-attenuation band, most often at the upper or lower pole, without adjacent hematoma, free fluid, or stranding.
Why it happens: Both cleft and laceration may appear as low-attenuation defects within splenic parenchyma.

column15: In blunt abdominal trauma, which solid organ is most commonly injured?
column16:
Fact: The spleen is the most commonly injured solid abdominal organ in blunt trauma.
Exam pivot: In blunt LUQ/abdominal trauma, default search should prioritize splenic injury while still surveying for associated abdominal and left thoracic injuries.

column15: What proportion of patients with polytrauma have splenic injuries according to the source?
column16:
Fact: Splenic injuries are present in 16-24% of patients with polytrauma.
Exam pivot: In polytrauma, splenic injury is common enough that subtle LUQ blood or sentinel clot should not be dismissed.

column15: How often are other injuries present with splenic injury according to the source?
column16:
Fact: Injury to other abdominal organs is present in 36.5%, and 80% of patients with splenic injury have extraabdominal injuries.
Exam pivot: Once splenic trauma is identified, actively search for additional abdominal, left thoracic, pancreatic-tail, mesenteric, hepatic, and diaphragmatic injuries.

column15: For splenic trauma, what is the key CT phase strategy?
column16:
Arterial phase: more sensitive for active extravasation or pseudoaneurysm.
Portal venous phase: more sensitive for parenchymal injury/laceration.
Delayed phase: differentiates active extravasation from pseudoaneurysm when a suspicious focus is seen.
Exam pivot: Use phase behavior to separate parenchymal injury from vascular injury.

column15: For splenic trauma, what CT attenuation thresholds support hemoperitoneum or perisplenic clot?
column16:
Hemoperitoneum: greater than 30 HU.
Perisplenic clot: greater than 45 HU.
Sentinel clot sign: highest-density blood localizes adjacent to the injury site.
Exam pivot: Dense blood adjacent to the spleen can reveal occult splenic injury even without a visible laceration.

column15: For splenic trauma, what sonographic appearance and limitation are source-supported?
column16:
US limitation: insensitive for parenchymal injury; subtle lacerations may be missed.
Lacerations: hypoechoic or isoechoic to splenic parenchyma and difficult to detect.
Hemoperitoneum: intraperitoneal fluid with low-level echoes; perisplenic clot may be echogenic.
Exam pivot: FAST can show fluid, but CT is needed to grade and characterize splenic injury.

column15: What is the 2018 AAST-OIS grading framework for splenic injury on CT?
column16:
Grade I: subcapsular hematoma <10% surface or capsular tear/superficial laceration <1 cm.
Grade II: subcapsular hematoma 10-50%, intraparenchymal hematoma <5 cm, or laceration 1-3 cm.
Grade III: subcapsular hematoma >50%/enlarging/ruptured, intraparenchymal hematoma >5 cm/expanding, or laceration >3 cm or involving trabecular vessels.
Grade IV: segmental/hilar vessel laceration with >=25% devascularization or contained vascular injury/active extravasation within capsule.
Grade V: shattered spleen, complete devascularization/hilar injury, or active extravasation extending beyond capsule into peritoneum.
Exam pivot: Vascular injury and extraperitoneal versus intraperitoneal active bleeding drive grade escalation.

column15: Which splenic trauma findings best predict need for intervention?
column16:
Best predictor: active arterial extravasation or pseudoaneurysm.
Stable patient: active extravasation, pseudoaneurysm, or hemoperitoneum may be managed with distal embolization; proximal splenic artery embolization can reduce splenic perfusion pressure while preserving parenchyma via collaterals.
Exam pivot: Vascular injury, not just laceration depth, is the major treatment-triggering imaging finding.

column15: For splenic trauma, when is nonoperative management preferred and when does it fail?
column16:
Preferred: whenever possible in hemodynamically stable patients without other injuries requiring surgery, including some higher-grade injuries.
Highest success: lower-grade injuries, especially grades I-III.
Failure risk: higher-grade injuries; grade V has a 75% failure rate.
Predictors of failure: age >55 years and other injuries.
Exam pivot: Hemodynamic stability allows splenic preservation, but grade V and vascular injury raise failure/intervention risk.

column15: For splenic trauma, what follow-up imaging strategy is source-supported?
column16:
Lower-grade injuries: repeat imaging generally does not change management and is not routinely recommended.
Higher-grade injuries: repeat imaging is recommended at 48-72 hours after presentation.
Exam pivot: Follow-up is risk-stratified; do not reflexively reimage every low-grade injury, but do not ignore high-grade injury risk.

column15: Why can FAST or DPL be falsely negative in splenic trauma?
column16:
Source-stated limitation: 29% of all patients and 40% of children with splenic injury may not have hemoperitoneum.
Exam pivot: A negative FAST/DPL does not exclude splenic injury, especially in children; CECT is the best diagnostic tool when clinical concern persists.

column15: What report element should be included for traumatic splenic injury?
column16:
Include the AAST-OIS splenic injury grade in reports detailing splenic traumatic injury.
Exam pivot: The grade communicates injury severity and helps guide management, especially when vascular injury is present.

column15: What CT features distinguish common mimics of splenic laceration?
column16:
Splenic cleft: thin fissure-like polar low-attenuation band without adjacent hematoma/free fluid/stranding.
Splenic infarct: wedge-shaped peripheral low attenuation, usually without surrounding free fluid or hematoma unless ruptured.
Splenic abscess: rounded irregular low-attenuation collection with infection signs; gas is highly specific when present.
Exam pivot: Adjacent traumatic blood and irregular branching/deep defects favor laceration/fracture.
